Article.ArticleTitle: Hermann J. Muller's research swan song: His long-term radiation dose-rate mutation investigations yielded no research publications, leaving questions of scientific misconduct.

Abstract.AbstractText: This paper evaluates the final major research effort of Hermann J. Muller, Nobel Laureate, to assess whether radiation displays a dose-rate process for radiation-induced germ cell mutation. Muller tried to extend the dose-rate findings of William Russell, Oak Ridge National Labs (ORNL), in male/female mice with his Drosophila fruit fly model. The paper reveals that Muller had no success in clarifying the dose-rate question with five years of substantial funding from the US Atomic Energy Commission (AEC). Despite a prolific scientific publication career, Muller published no data-based research articles in this area despite massive efforts, while Russell was publishing dose-rate discoveries with mice. This paper assesses the scientific reasons for Muller's failure to develop reliable information on the dose-rate issue in Drosophila, an area now well characterized by multiple research teams. The paper describes Muller's research strategies, how he coped with failure, research uncertainty and funding agency concerns with his lack of progress, despite his status. The paper shows that a frustrated Muller blamed the lack of success on erroneous dosimetry provided by physicists at Brookhaven National Laboratory, an argument that was refuted even to the satisfaction of Muller's staff. Yet Muller would alter the research record with respect to confirmation of the accuracy of the physicist's dosimetry, leading to the assertion that Muller's final research actions involved research misconduct via falsification. Muller's career at the University of Indiana would end in a frustrating manner for both the sponsor (AEC) and Muller and, with a new accusation of research misconduct.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: School of Public Health and Health Sciences, Department of Environmental Health, Morrill I-N344, University of Massachusetts, Amherst, MA 01003 USA. Electronic address: edwardc@umass.edu.

If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
